rulings.Case Title: ROBERT RAGO VS. BASIL PLASTIRAS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem The Truth Of Matters And Genuineness Of Documents Specified In Requests For Admissions, Set Two, Admitted And Conclusively Established; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff Robert Rago
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 13, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANTS BASIL PLASTIRAS AND MICHAEL TERRIZZI, PLASTIRAS & TERRIZZI's Motion To Deem The Truth Of Matters And Genuineness Of Documents Specified In Requests For Admissions, Set Two, Admitted And Conclusively Established; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff Robert Rago Pro Tem Judge Mark Schreiber, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Per Order Shortening Time, opposition papers not expected to be filed until time for tentative rulings are due. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to mschreiber@csclaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/pjt

rulings.Case Title: CYNTHIA FOSS VS. PETER BENNETT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Peter Bennett'S Motion To Quash And-Or Modify Plaintiff Cynthia Foss Subpoena To First Republic Bank
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 13, 2015, Line 3, Defendant Peter Bennett'S Motion To Quash And-Or Modify Plaintiff Cynthia Foss Subpoena To First Republic Bank Pro Tem Judge Mark Schreiber, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. Defendant moves to quash a post-judgment deposition subpoena for business records issued to a third-party, First Republic Bank. Although third-party business records subpoenas are authorized by California's Civil Discovery Act, CCP section 2016.070 states that its provisions "appl[y] to discovery in aid of enforcement of a money judgment only to the extent provided in [CCP sections 708.010 through 709.030]." The statutory scheme for enforcing money judgments permits a judgment creditor to utilize only two procedures provided in California's Civil Discovery Act to obtain discovery. First, the creditor may (subject to certain limitations) propound to the judgment debtor written interrogatories in the manner provided by section 2030.010 et seq. of the Civil Discovery Act. ( 708.020.) Second, the creditor may (subject to certain limitations) demand that the judgment debtor produce and permit inspection of documents in the manner provided by section 2031.010 et seq. of the Civil Discovery Act. ( 708.030.) None of the other modes of discovery (such as third-party business records subpoenas governed by CCP section 2020.410 et seq.) are "provided in" sections 708.010 through 709.030; they are therefore unavailable. Further, the authority cited by plaintiff applies only in the context of a third-party subject to a court order for examination pursuant to CCP section 708.120 based on an affidavit showing possession or control of the judgment debtor's property. This is not the case here. Accordingly, since the business records subpoena at issue here is not one of the procedures provided in CCP sections 708.010 through 709.030 to pursue collection of a judgment, plaintiff's attorney did not have authority to issue and serve the subpoena and the motion to quash is granted. No sanctions are awarded. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to mschreiber@csclaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.

rulings.Case Title: MARYANNE MORCH VS. SURGICAL CARE AFFILIATES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 13 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT SURGICAL CARE AFFILIATES, LLC, GOLDEN GATE ENDOSCOPY CENTER, LLC MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication. (Part 1 of 2). The motion for summary judgment is granted at to GGEC. The undisputed evidence demonstrates that plaintiff and GGEC do not have an employment relationship. Therefore, plaintiff cannot establish causes of action 1, 2, 3, 4, and 7. Plaintiff also cannot establish causes of action 5 and 6 because the common interest privilege of CC 47(c) bars the claims. The motion for summary judgment is denied as to SCA. The motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. The motion is denied as to causes of action 1, 2, 4, and 7. While defendant has proffered a legitimate reason for the adverse employment actions (Seidler dec., pars. 20 and 24), plaintiff has presented substantial evidence showing that the reasons were pretextual. At par. 10 of her declaration, plaintiff states that Dr. Aron initiated the conversation regarding Ms. Caramucci and Mr. Vidal also participated in the conversation. Mr. Vidal (who is younger) was not disciplined, however. Ms. Beck declares at paragraph 6 of her declaration that she too complained to Dr. Aron regarding Ms. Caramucci, but she was not disciplined. Ms. Beck is also younger and the trier of fact can infer age discrimination from the disparate treatment. In addition, Kornach stated that she never told Seidler that plaintiff refused to take a break. (Kornack Depo, 125: 18-20). There is also evidence that the reason for the termination was pretextual. At paragraph 11 of her declaration, plaintiff declares that Ms. Kornach never instructed her to start at 8:30 a.m. Mr. Kelly at paragraph 3 of his declaration states that he witnessed the conversation and corroborates plaintiff's version of the facts. The trier of fact could conclude that defendant's proffered reasons for the probation and termination are unworthy of credence. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding the retaliation cause of action. As stated, the trier of fact could find that defendant's proffered reasons for its conduct are pretextual. The adverse employment actions occurred shortly after plaintiff's protected activity. There is evidence of a causal nexus. Mr. Seidler admits that plaintiff was the only person that he witnessed with a copy of the letter. (Seidler's depo, 122:10-24). Plaintiff also mailed the letter to him in an envelope with her own handwriting. (Morch depo., 173:1-2). The court denies summary adjudication on the intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action because the claims of discrimination and retaliation can support the claim. FEHA is a cumulative remedy that does not displace the common law. The court denies summary adjudication on the punitives claim. Defendant failed to maintain its initial burden of production. In addition, the Shill deposition at page 17 indicates that Mr. Seidler was responsible for 3-4 of SCA's centers and a trier of fact could infer that he was a managing agent that could shape corporate policy. The court grants summary adjudication on causes of action 5 and 6 (defamation) because the person who uttered the defamation was not SCA's employee and as stated, the statement was privileged. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. (Continued to Part 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: MANUEL TORRES VS. AUSTIN EDWARDS HILLS 2004 TRUST et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO AMENDED ANSWER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 13 2015, Line 8, PLAINTIFF MANUEL TORRES DEMURRER TO AMENDED ANSWER TO COMPLAINT. The demurrer is overruled in part and sustained in part. The motion to strike is granted in part. The demurrer is sustained with 10 days leave to amend as to the following affirmative defenses: (21) privilege and justification - the answer fails to specify which causes of action this affirmative defense applies to as required by CRC 2.112; and (23) fraud - the answer does not plead fraud with the requisite particularity. The demurrer to the following affirmative defense is sustained without leave to amend: (46) failure to state representative B&P claim - there are no class or representative allegations in the complaint and therefore this affirmative defense is inapplicable. The demurrer to all other affirmative defenses is overruled. The motion to strike is granted as follows: (36) absence of disability - stricken as to the 4th cause of action; (37) failure to communicate disability - stricken as to the 4th cause of action; (39) undue burden - stricken as to the 4th cause of action; and (42) frivolous, unreasonable and lacking foundation - stricken as to the 5th through 12th causes of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.
